data.title: The Power Of Patience - 3 Of My Favorite Dividend Growth Stocks
data.description:
Introduction

On February 14, I wrote an article covering my entire portfolio, which you can access here if you like. So far, it has close to 300 comments, which is one of the highest numbers I have encountered in a very long time.

So, to everyone who participated, thank you!

As I have said in so many articles, the comment section is a great source of information for a wide range of people, including me. For example, many readers brought up fantastic questions, highlighting my lack of technology exposure and the fact that I do not own many stocks with high, double-digit dividend growth.

These comments are entirely justified. As I am 29 and have no plans of retiring anytime soon, I technically have decades of dividend growth ahead of me - God willing, of course.

With that in mind, one of the reasons why I have increasingly focused on higher-yielding stocks in recent months is because I believe we will see a rotation from growth to value stocks.

This is what I wrote in an article titled "Buckle Up Buttercup: My Warning To Dividend Investors" (emphasis added):

I have been concerned about lofty market valuations, persistent above-average inflation, and geopolitical risks, and the Fed's recent actions have, unfortunately, reinforced my concerns.

The market's negative reaction to the rate cut, especially the upward revision of future rate expectations, signals a growing acceptance of "higher for longer."

While this situation presents challenges, I believe it also creates opportunities.

My focus is shifting towards high-quality cyclical dividend growth stocks, which offer compelling valuations, healthy balance sheets, pricing power(!!!) and attractive yields, despite the current economic uncertainty.

On top of that, I decided to spend more time on income opportunities because many of my readers are retired.

Having said all of this, in this article, I'll focus on the "growth" aspect of dividend growth. Three stocks with low yields, fantastic business models, and a high probability of elevated long-term (dividend) growth.

I own two of them and will likely add the third one this year, as I believe I need to add technology exposure.

So, without further ado, let's get to it!

Reasons To Buy Dividend Growth

More often than not, when I cover low-yielding dividend stocks, some people get very angry with me. They say that I shouldn't call a dividend stock with a low yield a dividend stock because one needs to buy a lot of it in order to generate a decent income.

That is correct - at least the income part.

After all, if we invest $10,000 in a dividend stock with a 0.4% yield, we get $40 in annual dividends. That won't even get you groceries for a week.

So, regardless of what I'm going to say next, investors who need to squeeze the most income out of every dollar invested won't like any of the picks I present.

However, everyone with a long-term horizon and some capital for dividend growth stocks may benefit, as dividend growth and high-yield strategies both have merits.

In 4Q24, ProShares wrote an article about how dividend growth stocks can add a more balanced approach, as it noted the following:

The immediate gratification of high-yield income may be tempting. But it is only one component of total return—the larger part being capital appreciation. It is capital appreciation that has delivered most of the market’s returns over time. - ProShares

As we can see below, since at least 2005, consistent dividend growers have "crushed" the Dow Jones U.S. Select Dividend Index.

Even better, regardless of the interest rate environment, dividend growth has been the place to be, creating an "all-weather" approach for investors.

With that said, there's a reason I brought up the need for a longer-term time horizon. After all, this strategy requires patience when the goal is to generate income.

As we can see below, the average S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrat (used as a benchmark for dividend growth stocks) had an annual dividend growth rate of 7.8%. This is roughly 300 basis points above the average high-yield growth rate.

Over time, this causes dividend growth stocks to offer a higher yield on cost than high-yield stocks, making these stocks superior picks for both income and growth. But then again, you'll need some patience to benefit from both of these things.

Based on that context, dividend growth isn't a topic we neglect.

Now, let's dive into the picks, which include one stock to finally add technology exposure to my portfolio.

Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) - A FANG Stock I Like

Alphabet is a company I have discussed in a number of comments, as quite a few readers brought it up. I also highlighted it in a few multi-ticker articles in recent months, as I have become a fan of the company.

For starters, the company behind the Google brand yields just 0.4% after declaring its first-ever dividend on April 25, 2024. Back then, it also announced an additional buyback boost of $70 billion, which is more than the entire market cap of the smallest 350 S&P 500 members(!).

I believe Alphabet is one of the best growth stocks on the market, as it continues to show the world it's capable of elevated growth in high-demand areas.

For example, in 4Q24, the company generated $96 billion in revenues, 12% more compared to the prior-year quarter. This was supported by an operating margin of 32% (up from 27%) and growth across the board - even in its Google Search segment, which many feared would suffer from AI competition.

Even better, the company is using AI to its advantage, with Gemini 2.0 models being integrated across its products. While AI competition is fierce, the company has a HUGE installed base, as more than 90% of internet users use Google Search. YouTube has more than 2 billion monthly users, and its Android operating system is used by more than 3 billion people.

In order to fuel growth, the company is expected to invest roughly $75 billion in CapEx this year, using AI to enhance its existing services. I added emphasis to the quote below:

Third, our Products and Platforms put AI into the hands of billions of people around the world. We have seven Products and Platforms with over 2 billion users and all are using Gemini. That includes Search, where Gemini is powering our AI overviews. People use Search more with AI overviews and usage growth increases over time as people learn that they can ask new types of questions. This behavior is even more pronounced with younger users who really appreciate the speed and efficiency of this new format. - GOOGL 4Q24 Earnings Call

With that said, the valuation is fascinating, as Alphabet trades at just 22.7x earnings. This is despite high growth expectations, as analysts expect 11% EPS growth in 2025 to be followed by 14% and 15% growth in 2026 and 2027, respectively. This is according to the FactSet data in the chart below.

Using its 10-year average P/E ratio of 24.9x, we get a total return outlook of 17% per year.

I believe Alphabet is so "cheap" because the market isn't convinced AI isn't a Google killer. If Alphabet fails to innovate, that could indeed be the case.

However, I am willing to bet on the opposite, which could lead to substantial returns and elevated dividend growth. After all, it has a payout ratio of a mere 10% and a fantastic growth outlook, even in an environment of elevated uncertainty.

Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL) - A High-Conviction Investment Of Mine

Last year, one of the stocks I bought was Old Dominion Freight Line, a company I had on my radar for many years.

Although it yields just 0.5%, it has a payout ratio of less than 20% and a five-year dividend CAGR of 36%. Moreover, since announcing a dividend in 2017, it has bought back 14% of its shares. While that may not seem like a lot, ODFL is the only company in its industry buying back stock.

Even better, ODFL is the only company with a positive net cash balance, meaning it has more cash than gross debt. As we can see below, the company has $109 million in cash and a mere $40 million in net debt, down from $60 million at the end of 2023.

The engine behind these fantastic numbers is its less-than-truckload ("LTL") business model. Unlike truckload operators, the company moves shorter distances of roughly 915 miles per trip - on average.

LTL is so powerful because it relies on building a superior network of service centers that connect shippers to their customers. ODFL has done a terrific job, as it has built a network of more than 260 service centers in 48 states. Most of these are owned by the company.

Because of its efficient investments, it has a superior service, which provides the company with satisfied customers and a lot of pricing power.

As a result, this network has allowed it to grow its revenue by 12% per year since 2002, almost entirely organic, meaning no major M&A was involved to achieve this.

This has boosted its market share from 2.9% in 2002 to 12.0% in 2023.

Unfortunately, right now, the company is suffering from cyclical weakness. In 4Q24, revenue declined by 7.3% due to lower volumes. However, it still achieved a 99% on-time service ratio and a cargo claims ratio of less than 0.1%.

It also maintained an operating ratio of slightly less than 76.0%, which is a stunning number, as it means that just 76% of its revenues were used to operate the business (operating expenses).

While that may seem like a lot, it's a mind-blowing number in an industry where most operate with operating ratios above 90%. In the truckload business, operating ratios close to 100% are not uncommon.

Because of these issues, dividend growth in February was "just" 7.7%.

Going forward, I'm a lot more upbeat, as I expect a cyclical growth bottom in the United States, as I wrote in a recent article. That's also why I expect dividend growth to rise substantially in the years ahead, as LTL is a big part of the domestic industrial economy and a major winner of economic re-shoring.

Because of a poor performance in 2024 and high expectations, ODFL is the only stock of the three in this article that's not cheap.

Currently, ODFL trades at 37.5x earnings. The good news is that even in this environment, analysts expect 19% and 12% EPS growth in 2026 and 2027, respectively, growth rates that blow most cyclical stocks out of the water.

Personally, I expect actual numbers to come in higher, as a potential cyclical growth bottom would fuel pricing power and total volumes. In 2021 and 2022, this led to 57% and 37% EPS growth, respectively.

While ODFL is the wrong choice for income-focused investors (like all stocks in this article), I am eagerly buying more stock during corrections, as it's one of my highest-conviction ideas.

Carlisle Companies (CSL) - We Just Bought A Lot More

Just like ODFL, Carlisle is a high-conviction dividend growth investment I just bought for my parents as well. In fact, due to the recent stock price decline, we doubled that position, as the CSL ticker is one of our favorite investments.

That's also why I have brought it up in a number of articles this year.

Our bullishness is based on a number of reasons, including its fantastic position in commercial and residential construction. These markets benefit from strong secular growth tailwinds, as the average commercial building is 55 years old, and residential construction has suffered from consistent supply shortages since the Great Financial Crisis.

Carlisle generates roughly 70% of its revenue from construction materials. The remaining 30% are generated through weatherproofing technologies. Slightly more than 60% of all sales are used for replacement and remodeling operations - mainly targeting commercial buyers in the United States.

Moreover, as we can see above, it has superior financials, as its return on invested capital is roughly 25%. This means the company puts capital to work in a very efficient way, which has been a big factor in growing its business.

By 2030, the company aims to generate at least $40 in adjusted earnings per share, supported by 5% average annual organic revenue growth and adjusted EBITDA margins of no less than 25%.

Additionally, the company has a track record of resilience, as we can see below. Even during the Great Financial Crisis and the 2020/2021 pandemic, it maintained solid revenues and was able to grow/maintain its favorable margin profile.

This has also benefited its dividend.

Although CSL yields just 1.1%, it has a subdued payout ratio of just 18.3% and a five-year CAGR of 15.5%. Last year, it hiked its dividend by roughly 18%.

Even better, the dividend has been hiked for 47 consecutive years, making it one of the fastest-growing soon-to-be Dividend Kings on the market.

All of this also explains why Carlisle has consistently beaten the S&P 500, as we can see below.

Currently, the stock price has entered some turmoil, as investors are pricing in cyclical headwinds:

These headwinds included higher interest rates, restricting lending conditions and unfavorable weather patterns. These factors negatively impacted sales and the results were below our midyear 2024 outlook. - CSL 4Q24 Earnings Call

The good news is that Carlisle sticks to its Vision 2030. I would even make the case that if the company were to abandon its target, it would be priced in, as CSL stock trades at a highly favorable valuation.

Currently, CSL trades at just 17.5x earnings, which is well below its ten-year average of 20.5x and comes with a favorable growth outlook, as analysts expect 11-13% average annual EPS growth through at least 2027.

If we incorporate a P/E normalization, we get a path to a $580 stock price, roughly 60% above the current price.

While none of these picks are suitable for investors in desperate need of income, I believe they all have what it takes to beat the market over time, supported by fantastic business models, a focus on shareholder value, and secular growth tailwinds.

Takeaway

In this article, I revisited my portfolio strategy and emphasized the importance of balancing high-yield and dividend-growth stocks.

While I've leaned toward higher-yielding stocks recently, I also recognize the value of low-yield, high-growth opportunities for long-term investors.

Stocks like Alphabet, Old Dominion Freight Line, and Carlisle Companies are perfect examples of this approach, as they offer strong business models, pricing power, and significant dividend growth potential.

Though these picks may not suit income-focused investors, they align with my belief in the power of patience and compounding.

Needless to say, as I continue to adapt my portfolio, I remain committed to finding opportunities that balance income, growth, and resilience in an uncertain market.

Risks To My Thesis

While I'm confident in my focus on dividend growth stocks, there are risks to consider.

A prolonged economic downturn could pressure cyclical stocks like Old Dominion and Carlisle, delaying growth. Additionally, Alphabet's heavy reliance on AI innovation leaves it vulnerable to competition and technological shifts.

Higher-for-longer interest rates could also weigh on valuations and consumer demand, impacting all three companies.

Lastly, dividend growth strategies require patience, as they simply do not have the payout that higher-yielding stocks have.

data.title: S&P 500 Rises As News Headlines Miss The Week's Biggest Story
data.description:
The market-moving headlines in the week were pretty bad for Wall Street bulls. A higher-than-expected consumer inflation report on Wednesday, 12 February 2025 would all but seem to have diminished the chances of even one rate cut during 2025. At least, that's the news the market-moving headlines proclaimed.

But by the end of the trading week, investor expectations for the outlook for interest rate cuts in 2025 changed to go in a different direction. The CME Group's FedWatch Tool closed out the week anticipating a quarter point rate cut to be announced after the Fed meets on 7 May (2025-Q2), about 12 weeks earlier than it forecast a week earlier. While that remains the only rate change expected in 2025, the FedWatch tool suggests another quarter point rate cut is likely in January 2026, which could move up into 2025-Q4 if the momentum for the change in expectations continues.

That change coincided with investors shifting their attention once again to 2025-Q4, which coincides with a 1.5% increase in the level of the S&P 500 over the previous week. The index reached 6,114.63 on Friday, 14 February 2025, just several points shy of its all-time record high of 6,118.71 from 23 January 2025. The latest update of the alternative futures chart tracks the trajectory of the S&P 500 changing with along with the changing investment horizon for investors.

Here are the week's market-moving headlines, in which the business news media missed the late breaking change in investor expectations for how the Federal Reserve will be setting the Federal Funds Rate in 2025 and beyond.

Monday, 10 February 2025

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Trump raises tariffs on aluminum, steel imports in latest trade war salvo
      • Where does the US get its steel and aluminum from?
      • US copper price premium soars to record after Trump tariff moves
      • US steel makers stocks rise, foreign peers fall on Trump tariff plans
      • Looming tariffs turned into a mad dash north for one US manufacturer
    • Oil gains nearly 2% despite trade war concerns
  • Fed officials expected to sit on hands for months before next interest rate move:
    • Fed to wait for months on next rate cut as tariffs risk inflation flare up: Reuters poll
  • Bigger trouble developing in China:
    • China to roll back clean power subsidies after boom
    • China's consumer inflation at 5-month high, producer deflation persists
  • Nasdaq, S&P, and Dow ended in the green as investors await inflation data
    • Gold bulls lock in on glittering milestone; $3,000/oz in sight

Tuesday, 11 February 2025

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Oil prices climb to 2-week high on supply worries, US tariffs check gains
      • EIA raises US oil production forecast for 2025
  • Fed officials say US economy is okie dokie and so are their monetary policies, so there's no need to rush to cut interest rates faster:
    • Fed's Powell, quizzed about trade, Musk, and bank safety, says economy is fine
      • Powell says there's no rush to ease more given strong economy
    • Fed's Williams says monetary policy is in right place to achieve Fed goals
  • Bigger trouble, stimulus developing in China:
    • Shares of Chinese steelmakers slide as Trump's fresh tariffs add uncertainty on exports
    • China's car sales post biggest drop in almost a year in January
  • Eurozone officials thinking about responding to US steel, aluminum tariffs:
    • EU readies response as Trump hikes steel and aluminium tariffs
      • Germany's trade surplus with US reaches new record
      • German exports rise, industrial production falls
  • Wall Street ends mixed on tariff drama, Fed comments; focus shifts to consumer inflation
    • Wall Street ends mixed as Coca-Cola gains and Tesla drops

Wednesday, 12 February 2025

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • US consumer inflation increases at fastest pace in nearly 1-1/2 years in January
      • Inflation heats up more than expected in January CPI
      • Hotter January inflation jolts markets, dimming rate cut hopes
      • Few mourn the US penny as Trump moves to kill it
    • Oil prices retreat after report of U.S. crude stockpile rise
    • US fourth-quarter 2024 earnings growth set to be highest in three years
  • Fed officials surprised by inflation, thinking about stalling more rate cuts:
    • Fed's Powell sees a ways to go on shrinking Fed holdings
    • US inflation jump puts Fed officials on notice as Powell testifies
      • Fed's Bostic: Not comfortable cutting rates again until there is more clarity on inflation, other policies
    • Hot Jan US CPI surprise argues for Fed staying in neutral
  • BOJ officials say they're going to aim at 2% inflation for Japan's economy:
    • BOJ to conduct monetary policy to achieve 2% inflation target, Governor Ueda says
    • BOJ governor says higher food prices in Japan may alter inflation expectations
  • ECB officials say they're excited to keep cutting Eurozone interest rates, have dark outlook for Eurozone economy:
    • ECB can keep cutting rates even if Fed takes it slow, Vujcic says
      • Reuters interview with ECB policymaker Vujcic
    • ECB's Nagel wary of blurry 'neutral' rate target, U.S. tariff damage
    • ECB's Villeroy reaffirms that Trump policies will likely be negative for economy
  • S&P 500 ends down as hot US inflation data hints at fewer rate cuts
    • Dividend attention grows as Wall Street digests higher inflation report

Thursday, 13 February 2025

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • US producer inflation trends higher; labor market remains stable
    • Trump outlines reciprocal tariff plan in latest bid to reshape trade on his terms
      • Trump's memo outlining reciprocal tariffs plans
    • US power companies increase data center demand spending as DeepSeek fears wane
  • Higher inflation pushing back timing of Fed officials' expected 2025 rate cut:
    • Fed seen likely policy hold until September; Traders add bets on July cut
  • BOJ officials expected to keep delivering interest rate hikes because of inflation in Japan:
    • Japan's wholesale inflation jumps, reinforcing BOJ rate-hike bets
    • Most economists expect BOJ to hike rates in latter half of 2025, survey shows
  • Nasdaq, S&P close up 1% as Trump tariff directive staves off new immediate levies
    • Wall Street ends higher after Trump unveils tariff plan
    • Can the S&P 500 pull off a three-peat 20%+ year? Wells Fargo says, 'In short, no'

Friday, 14 February 2025

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Oil edges lower as potential Ukraine peace deal eases supply worries
    • US retail sales post biggest drop in nearly two years amid winter freeze
    • US manufacturing output falls in January on weak motor vehicle production
  • Fed officials to address criticism of how they measure inflation by becoming less clear about it; also say they want to go slower on rate cuts:
    • Fed may only nuance much-maligned ‘averaging’ strategy
    • Fed's Logan calls for caution on rate cuts, even if inflation cools
  • Bigger trouble, stimulus developing in China:
    • Chinese exporters brace for 'rat race' in shift away from US
    • China January bank lending hits record high on policy stimulus
    • China's property loan contraction eases after government support
  • Dow, S&P, and Nasdaq closed mixed as markets snapped a two-week losing streak

The Atlanta Fed's GDPNow tool's projection of what real GDP growth will be in the 2025-Q1 dropped from last week's +2.9% to +2.3% on 14 February 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Lululemon Stock Could Be A Lemon (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Thesis

Lululemon Athletica Inc. (NASDAQ:LULU) stock has soared over 50% in the past six months but in the below analysis, I determine that this run could be over. The technicals have been determined to be net bearish as the moving averages and indicators show concerning signs. The fundamentals were overall resilient for their Q3, but sluggish revenue growth is a major concern. Updated guidance shows that revenue growth is expected to accelerate modestly, but EPS growth is likely to slow substantially. In addition, after evaluating the P/S and P/E ratios, I find that the stock is modestly overvalued at current levels. Therefore, since the technicals are heavily bearish and fundamental setup is overall unattractive, I initiate Lululemon stock at a sell rating.

Technicals

Charting

Trends

Lululemon stock was in an uptrend that started back in early October but on the most recent trading day the stock plunged below the trend line on relatively high volume and so the uptrend may no longer be intact. Investors should wait for a more pronounced breakdown to draw conclusions, however. This former uptrend line could be a source of resistance moving forward.

Support

There are three important support levels on this chart. The nearest one is right at the current stock price of around 366. This level was major support in December and January and so the stock has potential of bouncing here. The next support level is an upside gap back in December. Its importance is high since the volume on that day was extraordinary. The last support area is relatively distant in the high 290s but was key support in late October and November and so therefore could be support if the stock slumps.

Resistance

In addition to the potential resistance of the former uptrend line, there is only one other resistance level. It is at around 405 and represents major resistance back in December and January and so this level could act as resistance on major rallies in the stock.

Takeaway

Overall, I would say that the chart is a quite neutral one in its signalling. The stock broke out of its uptrend but that doesn't necessarily mean that a downtrend is developing. In addition, there are also many support levels below the stock and so major declines are unlikely at this point.

Moving Averages

Simple Moving Average (SMA)

The 50 day SMA had a bullish golden cross with the 200 day SMA in December as a clear signal of longer term strength in the stock. The gap between the SMAs has also expanded to its widest margin yet, showing that longer term bullishness is still on the rise. However, the stock broke below the 50 day SMA on its previous trading day and so there is definitely some near term weakness.

Exponential Moving Average (EMA)

The 9 day EMA just had a bearish crossover with the 21 day EMA as another signal of near term weakness in the stock. The stock has plunged far below both of the EMAs and so the gap between the EMAs is expected to widen substantially to reflect the greatly increased near term bearish momentum.

Bollinger Bands

The stock broke below the midline earlier this month as a indication of weakness in the stock. It is also a clear confirmation that the uptrend in the stock in no longer intact. Currently, the stock is trading at the lower band after the plunge on the previous trading day and so an oversold bounce could occur in the near future.

Takeaway

I believe there are mixed signals is this moving average analysis with the SMAs remaining strong, but for the near term things are clearly bearish for Lululemon. The stock broke below the 50 day SMA, the EMAs just had a bearish crossover, and the Bollinger Bands confirms the end of the uptrend.

Indicators

Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD)

The MACD had a bearish crossover with the signal line earlier this month. The gap between the lines has been growing since the crossover and since the trajectory of the MACD is worsening, the histogram is firmly bearish and reflects accelerating bearish momentum. There is also longer term bearish divergence in the MACD as it was at a much lower level during the late January peak in the stock than back in at the December peak.

Relative Strength Index (RSI)

The RSI is currently at 34.12 after it recently plunged below the 50 level. This indicates that the bears are in solid control of the stock as of now. Like the MACD, the RSI also reflects negative divergence for the late January high in the stock as the indicator failed to best levels seen at the peak in December.

Stochastics

The stochastics had a bearish crossover to start February as the %K broke below the %D line within the overbought 80 zone. In addition, after the crossover, the %K bounced off the %D and failed to break back above and so the bears have been highly resilient as of late. Both lines also plunged below the 50 level earlier this month as further evidence that the bears are in clear control of the stock.

Takeaway

In my view, the indicators are clearly bearish for the stock in both the near and long terms. For the near term, the MACD shows accelerating bearish momentum, the RSI plunged below 50 to show strong bearish control, and the stochastics also had multiple bearish signals. For the longer term, both the MACD and RSI showed negative divergence and so investors should question the long term strength of the stock.

Fundamentals

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Lululemon released their 2024 Q3 earnings back in December and generally showed resilient results. They reported revenues of $2.4 billion, up 9% YoY and a GAAP diluted EPS of $2.87, up from $1.96 YoY. As you can see in the above chart, overall revenue growth has been relatively weak with Q3 only representing a very minor improvement over the previous quarter and does not show that the downtrend in revenue growth is coming to an end. For EPS, growth is at quite strong levels if you disregard the early 2024 spike. Both revenue and EPS also beat expectations as revenue beat by $39.27 million and GAAP EPS beat by $0.15, showing healthy performance versus consensus estimates. In terms of margins, Lululemon showed promising improvements. Gross margin was reported to be 58.5%, up 150 bps YoY and operating margin was 20.5%, up 520 bps YoY, showing that the company has improved their efficiency by a fair amount.

Guidance

Lululemon provided 2024 Q4 guidance in this earnings report but recently, they issued updated guidance in January. They are now expecting revenues of between $3.56 billion and $3.58 billion, up from the previous guidance range of $3.475 billion and $3.510 billion. For 2023 Q4, they had revenues of $3.2 billion and so the updated guidance implies a revenue growth rate of between 11.3% and 11.9%. This would represent a modest acceleration in revenue growth for Q4. They also provided diluted EPS guidance ranging from $5.81 to $5.85, up from the previous guidance of between $5.56 and $5.64. Since diluted EPS was $5.29 in 2023 Q4, this guidance shows that EPS growth will be between 9.8% and 10.6%. This is quite a steep deceleration in growth versus Q3. Overall, I believe the updated guidance shows significant improvement over the previous guidance but was still mixed as a whole as it shows that revenue growth may accelerate but EPS growth is expected to slow substantially for Q4.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio (P/S)

The P/S ratio is at below average levels in relation to the past three years. However, its current 4.507 reading represents major multiple expansion from the 2024 lows of around 3.0. I believe this ratio shows overvaluation in Lululemon stock. As discussed above, revenue growth is currently very near three year lows and while the guidance shows some modest acceleration in growth for Q4, it would still be very near multiyear lows. While the P/S is also below average, the multiple expansion in the second half of 2024 seems to be unjustified and detached from the fundamental growth of the company. Currently, the Consumer Discretionary sector's median P/S ratio is only 0.99 and so Lululemon is trading at hefty premium to peers. While its strong brand identification could justify some premium, an over 300% premium is likely unjustified according to the financials.

Price To Earnings Ratio (P/E)

The P/E ratio is currently quite far below average levels of the past few years. It currently has a reading of 26.49 and I believe it shows around fair valuation for Lululemon. As discussed above, currently EPS growth is quite robust but the guidance shows that this growth is expected to slow substantially in Q4. However, the implied growth of around 10% would not be an out of the ordinary weak figure as the company has experienced similar levels of growth in many periods throughout the past few years. Since the P/E ratio is currently far below average, there is potential that Lululemon could be in fact slightly undervalued relative to historic financial results. However, Lululemon is still trading at a premium to its consumer discretionary peers as the sector's median P/E is 19.97. This premium is much more reasonable than for the P/S ratio and only shows modest overvaluation.

Takeaway

I conclude that Lululemon stock is modestly overvalued at current levels. The P/S clearly shows overvaluation both relative to growth and to peers. However for the P/E ratio, the stock was shown to be perhaps slightly undervalued according to growth but slightly overvalued relative to peers. Therefore the P/E generally reflects fair valuation. When considering the P/S and P/E together, they show modest overvaluation.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, I have determined that the technicals are net bearish for Lululemon stock. While the chart was mainly net neutral, the moving averages and indicators show concerning signals. For the fundamentals, Q3 revenue growth was worrying and guidance shows that while revenue growth may accelerate modestly, EPS growth could fall significantly. As discussed above, the P/S and P/E multiples together show modest overvaluation in the stock, leading me to believe that the fundamental setup is currently unattractive. Therefore, since the technicals are bearish and the stock is overvalued, I believe Lululemon deserves a sell rating at this point.

data.title: Harding Loevner Emerging Markets Equity Q4 2024 Report
data.description:
Market Review

Emerging markets (EMs) were having a fine 2024 for most of the year. Investors were emboldened by improving growth prospects and falling interest rates, surging growth in artificial intelligence, and a rebound in China, with equities rising from inexpensive valuations. That changed in the fourth quarter. Donald Trump’s election focused attention on both the prospects of significant changes in US policies affecting trade and also the inflationary outlook, with interest rates potentially remaining higher for longer, as the Federal Reserve hinted in its December meeting. After falling 8% in the final quarter, EMs ended 2024 up 8%. While they outperformed developed markets outside of the US (the MSCI World ex US was up 5%), both lagged the US, the star of the year (the MSCI USA Index was up 25%), in part as the dollar strengthened 9% on a trade-weighted basis.

MSCI EM Index Performance (USD %)

Sector

4Q 2024

China rebounded about 20% on the year, after three consecutive years of equities declines during what has become the country’s worst economic downcycle since the late 1990s. The pressure of that downturn on people led to an acute rise in societal tension. In mid-September, after what had already been an unusual series of public attacks nationwide, local businessmen in Hunan apparently assassinated the province’s finance head; dozens of people have been killed in separate mass attacks since then, a highly visible break in social stability in a country where such crimes are rare.

Those incidents seemed to change the minds of policymakers who had previously been reluctant to undertake major stimulus programs. In late September, they finally launched their biggest coordinated stimulus package since the global financial crisis, and followed up with details throughout the fourth quarter. These included the first shift to an easier monetary policy stance in 14 years, as well as a US$1.4 trillion program to use central-government funds to shore up the balance sheets of local governments, some of which could no longer even afford to pay teachers and police on a regular basis after losing a key source of revenues—selling land to property developers—amid the prolonged decline in the real-estate market.

But despite a flurry of economic policy announcements and press conferences in subsequent weeks, concrete details remained thin, leading markets to decline in October. Still, for the fourth quarter as a whole, despite the guessing game on policy and repeated promises by Trump to raise tariffs on China-made products to 60% or higher, Chinese equities outperformed broader EMs by a hair as investors continued to expect more stimulus.

Donald Trump’s election focused attention on both the prospects of significant changes in US policies affecting trade and also the inflationary outlook.

Across the strait in Taiwan, markets closed the year up 35% as semiconductor and other technology companies, including many suppliers to Nvidia (NVDA), saw rapid growth in demand as key enablers of broader usage of AI. South Korea, despite a sizable technology sector, fell 23% for the year, largely in the fourth quarter. Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF), its largest company, disappointed across several areas of its business. More dramatically, the country’s president, Yoon Suk Yeol, threw the political system into turmoil after invoking martial law for the first time in 45 years, an apparent attempt to dissolve the opposition-controlled legislature that had been trying to impeach him. Instead, that legislature, after overturning his declaration, ended up impeaching both Yoon and his successor; all this worsened the policymaking deadlock that had already defined Yoon’s administration. India closed up 12%, after its stock-market rally reversed in the fourth quarter, reacting to its securities regulator’s introduction of tighter policies to curb retail investor-driven speculation in the derivatives market, as well as slowing growth, which weighed on equity valuations that had been based on lofty expectations.

Latin America performed the worst by far, with Brazil down 29% and Mexico down 27% for the year. In Brazil, the government’s much-anticipated fiscal package failed to curb a persistent fiscal deficit, leading to higher interest-rate expectations and dramatic weakening in both its stock market and its currency, which contributed to a 12% slide for the real in dollar terms in the fourth quarter. In Mexico, investors reacted to Trump’s threats of higher tariffs, along with the prospect of Mexico’s judiciary losing its independence under its new administration’s plans to choose judges, including on its supreme court, by popular vote.

By sector, Information Technology outperformed for the year, up 21%, led by AI-enabling hardware makers in Taiwan that are represented disproportionately in that sector. Communication Services and Consumer Discretionary also did well, dominated by companies in China that rebounded on expectations of greater stimulus. Materials declined the most, by 20%, as commodity products saw weaker end demand, exacerbated by China’s ailing economy. Consumer Staples also underperformed across markets.

Latin America performed the worst by far, with Brazil down 29% and Mexico down 27% for the year.

By style factors, higher-quality companies underperformed significantly for the full year, particularly in China: the MSCI EM Quality Index lagged the overall MSCI EM Index by 5.7 percentage points, largely because of an unprecedented 25 percentage point underperformance by the MSCI China Quality Index against the broader MSCI China Index. In part, this reflected continued outperformance by high dividend-yielding state-owned companies in China amid falling growth expectations and record-low interest rates.

Performance and Attribution

The Emerging Markets Equity composite fell 8.1% gross of fees during the fourth quarter, modestly wider than the 7.8% loss of the MSCI Emerging Markets Index.

Holdings in IT and Financials were the primary causes of underperformance. In IT, Aspeed, a Taiwanese company specializing in fabless integrated-circuit design, faced concerns over the competitive threat from a smaller rival, particularly when vying for business from high-profile customers. Our underweight in Taiwanese semiconductor chip manufacturer TSMC (TSM) also hurt. Poor returns in IT were partially mitigated by IT services business EPAM, which reported solid third-quarter results and issued a positive growth outlook.

In Financials, our positions in Hong Kong-based AIA Group (OTCPK:AAGIY) and India’s HDFC Life detracted. Both have reported decent growth this year, but AIA’s stock may have been pressured by economic weakness in China, an important growth area for the company. Margins weakened at HDFC Life, which also faced concerns over potential new regulations on insurance sales through banks.

Relative returns were helped by our significant underweight in Materials (the worst-performing sector this quarter) and good stocks in Energy and Real Estate. In the former, Tenaris (TS), which makes steel pipes used in oil and gas production, has seen a nascent recovery in prices for its products in the US. In Real Estate, UAE-based Emaar Properties has been generating strong earnings, bolstered by sustained demand for Dubai’s real estate.

This information is supplemental to the composite GIPS Presentation.

Investors also cheered the announcement of a significantly higher dividend payout by the company.

By geography, Taiwan was the primary source of underperformance due to Aspeed and TSMC. Emaar boosted returns in the Middle East. A number of positive contributors in the quarter were EM companies listed in developed markets, notably Tenaris (listed in Italy, but with major operations in Argentina), EPAM (US listed, but with operations increasingly diversified across emerging markets), and Globant (GLOB, another US-listed IT services business but with operations focused on Latin America). All three did well, in large part due to rebounding demand from customers.

The composite significantly lagged the index over the trailing 12 months, also primarily due to our investments in IT and Financials. Despite its strong returns in the fourth quarter, EPAM’s stock was under pressure most of the year due to concerns about slowing demand for IT projects. The portfolio’s underweight in TSMC and overweight in Samsung Electronics also hurt returns in 2024.

UAE-based Emaar Properties has been generating strong earnings, bolstered by sustained demand for Dubai’s real estate.

In Financials, bank holdings GF Banorte (OTCQX:GBOOY) of Mexico and Bank Rakyat (OTCPK:BKRKY) in Indonesia detracted. In Mexico, investor worries about increasingly populist policies following Claudia Sheinbaum’s decisive victory in the June presidential election—as well as Trump’s victory in the subsequent election in the US, Mexico’s main trading partner—hurt Banorte and other stocks in this market. In Brazil, financial services company XP fell due to the threat of higher-for-longer interest rates in the country.

We outperformed in Real Estate thanks to Emaar Properties. Our strong returns in Energy this year were primarily due to our sale of Russia’s Lukoil after receiving a credible and reasonable offer for the shares; we had priced the portfolio position at effectively zero since March 2022 due to an inability to trade the shares.

Perspective and Outlook

Donald Trump’s November victory brought with it the fear of a shakeup in international geopolitics and a reorientation of supply chains for EM companies. The incoming US president’s threats to boost tariffs and trade barriers loomed large on EM investors’ minds and led to a sharp decline in EM returns in the fourth quarter.

If all this seems familiar, it is. EMs endured a similar sharp decline eight years ago after Trump’s first victory. While developed-market stocks embraced the risks of “Trumponomics,” EM stocks bore the brunt of it in the immediate aftermath of the 2016 election, as his agenda of increased protectionism weighed on EM returns. China and Mexico were front and center in those concerns in 2016, with the former facing threats of severe trade-related restrictions while the latter was being targeted through a renegotiation of NAFTA.

We were skeptical back then that one person could scuttle the global supply-chain lines that had been built up over decades. Indeed, EMs had a strong rebound during the first year of Trump’s presidency in 2017. The MSCI EM benchmark rose 38%, with stocks in both China and Mexico doing well. Once again, we are skeptical of the market’s evaluation of the risks. Global trade is not going to just stop because of changes in US policy. What we do believe will happen, however, is that global trade will continue to shift, as indeed it has shifted since Trump’s first term. And that shift will bring with it a different set of opportunities.

If all this seems familiar, it is. EMs endured a similar sharp decline eight years ago after Trump’s first victory.

The EM landscape outside of China is different from eight years ago. In many ways it is better. Looking at current macroeconomic conditions first, five-year average inflation rates are significantly lower now compared with corresponding numbers back in 2016, despite the spike in inflation during the pandemic years, indicating lesser sustained pressure on consumer purses now. Tight monetary policy across most EMs (with the notable exception of Brazil) has managed to keep inflation from spiking. As EM central banks are expected to cut interest rates over the next few years, we expect domestic consumption and economic growth in these countries to get a boost. While China continues to maintain a healthy current account surplus, current account balances of other important export-oriented EMs such as Mexico, Taiwan, South Africa, and Indonesia are significantly better than they were eight years ago, thus providing these economies with a better cushion against volatility in global trade.

There has been a lot of talk over the last few years about deglobalization, and Chinese exports to the US have in fact fallen from their peak due to trade restrictions. However, China’s exports as a percentage of overall global trade haven’t been affected that much, as global trade is simply being rerouted. China’s exports to Southeast Asian countries have increased significantly in the last few years, picking up the slack from US exports, and China remains the largest trading partner for about half of the countries in the Global South comprising more than 130 countries.

Indeed, despite its domestic problems, or rather precisely because of them, China must remain focused on trade. In the fourth quarter of 2024, President Xi Jinping attended economic summits at which he signed new trade deals and advocated for further globalization. In November he attended a ceremony in Peru to open the US$1.3 billion Chancay Port, which will significantly improve shipping times between China and Latin America. Boosting trade further opens up global markets for Chinese goods and brings desperately needed economic growth.

Global trade hit a record US$33 trillion in 2024, and while there is significant uncertainty about its path in the coming months and years, it clearly is going to remain significant. Companies will adjust to changes in the global trade environment by exploring the opportunities for diversification, strengthening supply chains, and utilizing new technologies. A recent survey of executives by The Economist Group found that only about a quarter of executives (26%) are working with fewer suppliers, nearly half (47%) are pursuing diversification, and virtually all (98%) are employing AI to improve the efficiency of their supply-chain operations. In other words, companies are and have been adapting to a changing world.

A good example of how companies are mitigating the risk of supply chain disruptions is China’s Midea Group, the largest home-appliance company globally and a leading global manufacturer of commercial heating, ventilation, and air conditioning ('HVAC'). Close to 40% of Midea’s 2023 revenue was derived outside of China. However, despite pressure on the stock price amid news related to potential US tariffs, we think that the impact on Midea’s earnings from another round of tariff increases is easily manageable. Direct exposure to US revenues is small, with only close to 10% of Midea’s revenues generated in the US per its 2023 filings.

Companies will adjust to changes in the global trade environment by exploring the opportunities for diversification, strengthening supply chains, and utilizing new technologies.

Moreover, Midea has a well-diversified global supply chain with 22 overseas manufacturing bases in 16 countries. Roughly 20% of Midea’s appliance-production capacity is located outside of mainland China, so production for the US market could be easily sourced from a manufacturing facility located in a country unaffected by potential tariffs. Midea also has strong bargaining power over its customers. Even in 2018, when US tariffs on imports of air conditioners and refrigerators from China had increased from 10% to 25%, Midea was able to pass through this higher tariff to its customers and maintain robust gross profit margins. We expect the company to be able to do the same if tariffs are raised again over the next four years.

Another portfolio holding that has benefited from expansion in its global footprint over the last decade is WEG (OTCPK:WEGZY), Brazil’s leading maker of electrical components, such as electric motors and transformers, with a fast-growing business in electricity generation and distribution for renewable sources of energy. WEG generated 53% of its revenue in 2023 outside its home market of Brazil, with close to 20% of total revenue generated in the US and the rest spread across Europe, Asia, and Africa. While this has helped expand the addressable market for WEG’s products and improved the company’s long-term growth outlook, it could lead to a perception of increased susceptibility to risk of trade disruptions for the company.

However, with 63 manufacturing sites across four continents and 17 countries, the company can promptly serve its customers with lower logistics costs, benefiting from the near-shoring trend that started during President Trump’s first term and intensified during the pandemic. WEG also has a vertically integrated manufacturing process that reduces its dependence on outside suppliers and reduces risk of supply-chain disruptions.

There are reasons for optimism outside of just these two examples. Like the case outlined for Midea above, when we look at other EM companies in our portfolio, long-term prospects for earnings and stock returns continue to look attractive. EM cash flow returns on investment (CFROI) have remained steady at close to 7% over the last nine years and remain well above CFROIs delivered by developed markets outside the US. The average portfolio holding also has a significantly higher quality profile now compared to 2016, while having a lower debt-to-equity ratio compared to the prior period, indicating lower balance sheet risk today.

Several of the reasons for the growth of EM companies in the last decade remain relevant and potent. Banks such as HDFC Bank (HDB) in India and Al Rajhi Bank in Saudi Arabia, along with Kenya’s leading mobile-payment company Safaricom, are expected to continue to benefit from increasing financial penetration in their domestic markets. Faster uptake of digitization, championed by digital payments and e-commerce companies such as MercadoLibre (MELI) in Latin America and Kaspi.kz (OTC:KSPI) in Kazakhstan, is also expected to lead to sustained increases in revenues and profits for those companies.

Then of course there are also new opportunities. Eight years ago, AI was mainly a science-fiction motif. Today rapid developments in AI have boosted the growth outlook for several EM companies. We own several semiconductor and hardware companies that are crucial to the AI manufacturing value chain. Our largest holding, TSMC, has more than 80% market share in leading-edge semiconductors globally and fabricates almost all of the chips designed by Nvidia. TSMC expects AI-related revenue to grow at a cumulative rate of 50% over the next five years. We also own power component maker Delta Electronics (OTCPK:DLEGF), which commands more than 50% market share in sophisticated power supplies needed for high-end servers. Then there are our three IT-Services companies, Globant, EPAM, and Tata Consultancy Services. Although it’s still early days for them, they have started working on business-transformation projects for clients aimed at helping those clients adopt and adapt AI for their own operations.

Finally, the relative valuation case for EMs compared to developed markets remains compelling.

When measuring valuation using current price-to-book multiples, the discount enjoyed by EMs (as measured by the MSCI EM Index) at the aggregate level compares favorably to the high current valuations of developed markets (MSCI World Index).

EMs are more economically resilient than they were eight years ago. Meanwhile many EM companies have greater growth opportunities and more attractive valuations. Given the unpredictability of policy in the future and the related macroeconomic risk that is being discounted in share prices, we remain focused on our fundamental research of companies with strong competitive advantages and good management teams that can adapt and outcompete through changing industry structures in increasingly unsettled times.

Emerging Markets’ Value Proposition

Source: Bloomberg. Data as of December 31, 2024.

Portfolio Highlights

During 2024 we established new positions in healthcare-related companies Clicks Group (OTCPK:CLCGY) and Raia Drogasil (OTCPK:RADLY), the leading pharmacy chains in South Africa and Brazil, respectively, as well as adding to our holding in Mindray, China’s leading medical-device company focusing on patient monitoring, in-vitro diagnostics and imaging. In the fourth quarter we made a new investment in Bupa Arabia, the leading health-insurance provider in Saudi Arabia.

Bupa Arabia, a part of the global Bupa group, has a dominant 45% share of the highly concentrated Saudi health-insurance market; its closest competitor, Tawuniya, a diversified insurance group, holds another 30%. Bupa Arabia’s dominant industry position provides greater bargaining power with healthcare service providers as well as the financial strength to invest in superior service quality and tailored insurance products. Offerings such as Bupa Parents and Bupa Family are attractive products for individual and corporate clients, especially when combined with value-added services such as health coaching and wellness programs. The company has also made investments in digital initiatives to enhance customer engagement and operational efficiency that help create a seamless user experience across its value chain, from policyholders to healthcare providers.

The Saudi government’s Vision 2030 agenda emphasizes improved healthcare in the kingdom, including greater private-sector participation, which supports the long-term growth of the industry. Health insurance is mandatory for all private-sector employees and their dependents, and greater enforcement of the law is creating a swell in demand for health-insurance products. We expect Buba’s revenues and profit growth to rise in the low-to-mid teens per year for the long term.

Bupa’s shares stalled for a period, which provided an opportunity for us to initiate a position at a time when the stock offered more that 30% upside to our estimate of fair value. Clearly investor sentiment has been affected by conflict in the region but also a short-term decline in Bupa’s market share versus Tawuniya that we expect will prove to be temporary.

Bupa Arabia’s dominant industry position provides greater bargaining power with healthcare service providers.

We sold three Chinese companies following the country’s sharp rally in the fall of 2024. One was StarPower, a company that makes specialized semiconductors used to help control the flow of electricity, such as in electric vehicles, solar inverters, and industrial machinery. It is a leader in its field, but we sold it after we met with the management of a newer competitor. We think it likely that competitor and other companies could drive up industry rivalry and threaten StarPower’s market share and growth prospects. The upshot was that the risks associated with our investment thesis had risen markedly, and with the stock trading 40% above the lows of September, we decided to sell.

We closed our ill-fated investment in LONGi, one of the largest makers of solar equipment in the world. The company’s dominance in the industry has not protected the stock, and a combination of a higher share price and a lowered long-term view of industry returns on capital led us to exit. Finally, we sold our holding in Hengli Hydraulic, China’s leading manufacturer of hydraulic components used in excavators. While we are confident that the companies we have in our portfolio can withstand the acrimony over global trade, we were less confident about Hengli’s ability to do so. The company has made significant investments in a new plant in Mexico, which it has struggled to bring online. That overhang and the uncertainty over how the Trump administration will approach trade with Mexico—as well as our perception of the limited upside after a three-month, 29% rally—prompted us to sell.

The sale of these three Chinese stocks has left the portfolio with exposure to China near that of the benchmark, a return to our neutral positioning entering 2024.

Harding Loevner’s Quality, Growth, and Value rankings are proprietary measures determined using objective data. Quality rankings are based on the stability, trend, and level of profitability, as well as balance sheet strength. Growth rankings are based on historical growth of earnings, sales, and assets, as well as expected changes in earnings and profitability. Value rankings are based on several valuation measures, including price ratios.

Companies held in the portfolio at the end of the year appear in bold type; only the first reference to a particular holding appears in bold. The portfolio is actively managed therefore holdings shown may not be current. Portfolio holdings should not be considered recommendations to buy or sell any security. It should not be assumed that investment in the security identified has been or will be profitable. To request a complete list of holdings for the past year, please contact Harding Loevner. A complete list of holdings at December 31, 2024 is available on page 9 of this report.

data.title: Forget SCHD: These Big Dividends Offer Higher Yields And Faster Growth
data.description:
Many dividend investors like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) because it combines a very attractive combination of current yield and proven long-term dividend growth. While investing in the S&P 500 (SPY) only gets you a 1.16% dividend yield, SCHD in contrast today offers a 3.58% dividend yield, which amounts to greater than three times what SPY's yield currently is.

On top of that, over the past decade, SPY has only grown its dividend at a 6.3% CAGR, and over the past 12 months, it has only grown its dividend at a 6.5% CAGR. In contrast, SCHD has grown its dividend at an 11.04% CAGR over the past 10 years and a whopping 12.23% CAGR over the past 12 months. As a result, for dividend investors, SCHD appears to be a slam dunk over SPY. On top of that, SCHD charges a meager 0.06% expense ratio, putting it in the same low-cost fund category as many other broadly diversified low-cost index funds, including SPY and others.

However, I do not invest in SCHD despite its clear allure for dividend investors. The reason for this is simply because I believe that by hand-picking individual high-yielding, high-dividend-growth stocks, I can achieve a higher current yield, a similar to higher dividend growth rate, and ultimately higher total returns in aggregate than I would achieve by simply owning SCHD with its roughly 100 blue-chip holdings. So far, this has proven to be the case for me, and I have no plans of changing my approach anytime soon.

In this article, I will share two such opportunities that currently offer a higher yield, higher dividend growth potential, and ultimately higher total return potential than SCHD, while also being very blue-chip, low-risk stocks.

Big Dividend Growth Stock #1

The first stock I am going to discuss today is Blue Owl Capital (OWL). It is an alternative asset management stock that has delivered exceptional total returns since it went public a few years ago, yet I still think it offers attractive total return potential moving forward.

In particular, management has guided for a $0.90 per share dividend in 2025, which amounts to a 3.8% dividend yield on a forward basis. That compares favorably to SCHD's 3.58% dividend yield.

However, where OWL really stands apart from SCHD is in the dividend growth category. Analysts forecast its dividend per share will grow at a 13.4% CAGR through 2029, which exceeds SCHD's past dividend growth metrics over any time span.

Moreover, I expect SCHD's dividend growth rate to slow moving forward, as it already has over the past three years, given its 9.87% dividend CAGR over the past three years, which is down significantly from its trailing 10-year and 5-year CAGRs of over 11%. On top of that, its top holdings include slower-growing blue chips like Coca-Cola (KO), Pfizer (PFE), and Verizon (VZ). While these stocks offer attractive dividend yields, they are unlikely to grow their dividends at rapid rates for the foreseeable future due to their slower revenue and earnings growth rates, which should weigh on SCHD's previously strong dividend growth pace.

Meanwhile, the reason to be optimistic that Blue Owl can achieve its aggressive dividend growth projections is that analysts forecast its earnings per share to grow at a 17.6% CAGR through 2029, which is even faster than its dividend growth pace.

This will be necessary because its current payout ratio is quite high, with a $0.90 dividend forecasted for 2025 compared to $0.92 per share in expected earnings for the year. Management has guided that its dividend growth rate will be slower than its earnings per share growth rate over the next several years as it works toward achieving a more normalized payout ratio of about 85%, which these respective projected growth rates indicate.

On top of that, management recently held an investor day where they predicted that the company would grow its assets under management over the next five years from about $251 billion today to over $500 billion in 2029. As a result, they expect fee-related earnings to grow at a 20%+ CAGR over that period. When accounting for low single-digit share dilution due to employee compensation each year, that brings the earnings per share to the consensus analyst estimate of about 17.6% growth.

Another reason to be bullish on OWL is that 91% of its fee-related management fees are driven by permanent capital, and its distributable earnings are 100% driven by fee-related earnings, which reduces its earnings volatility. On top of that, it has a strong investment-grade balance sheet and numerous avenues to drive growth across its alternative asset management business, giving it a relatively low-risk profile moving forward as well.

When you combine this very impressive growth runway with its 3.8% dividend yield, as well as the fact that OWL has been a much faster grower than its peers since its listing in 2021—with a 32% fee-related earnings (FRE) CAGR compared to the peer average of 18% FRE CAGR—and its valuation is on the lower end relative to peers like Blackstone (BX), KKR (KKR), and Brookfield Asset Management (BAM), OWL's total return profile looks quite attractive. In our view, while the broader sector is pretty richly priced, OWL is still in buy territory right now.

Big Dividend Growth Stock #2

The other big dividend stock I want to talk about today is MPLX (MPLX). While OWL is an asset-light, rapidly growing asset manager, MPLX is a more capital-intensive energy infrastructure company that issues a K-1 tax form and compensates investors for that extra hassle with a very juicy distribution yield, as well as a very promising distribution growth rate. In fact, I recently wrote a more thorough analysis of the company pointing out why I think it is arguably the best high-yield, high-growth investment right now. Since then, it has continued to soar higher and outpace the S&P 500.

However, it still remains an attractive opportunity today with a 7.5% next 12-month distribution yield, a very reasonable 10.6 times EV-to-EBITDA ratio, and a strong growth profile. Analysts forecast a roughly 10% distribution per unit CAGR through 2029, and management is also pursuing an aggressive pace of distribution hikes at the 12.5% level for the foreseeable future.

As management stated on its recent earnings call:

we are certainly optimistic that this level of distribution increase, like the 12.5% that our unitholders experienced in 2024, we'll be able to do that for years to come.

On top of that, its balance sheet is in strong shape with a BBB credit rating and a very low 3.1 times leverage ratio. It is also repurchasing units and investing opportunistically in attractive growth projects, especially in the natural gas and NGL infrastructure industry.

When you combine its double-digit total return profile, which is meaningfully higher than what we expect SCHD to generate moving forward, along with a distribution yield that is more than twice what SCHD currently offers, its blue-chip, investment-grade, highly contracted business model, and its low leverage ratio, MPLX stands out as an extremely attractive high-yield and high-growth investment that outperforms SCHD across all major metrics.

Investor Takeaway

As this article points out, while SCHD is certainly a worthy component of a dividend growth portfolio and is not a bad one-stop shop for dividend growth investors, several high-yield stocks offer both higher current yield and stronger dividend growth potential than SCHD. As a result, we prefer to build our portfolio out of these individual stocks rather than diversifying into a broadly diversified fund like SCHD at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Twilio: The Post-Earnings Dip Should Have Been Worse
data.description:
Introduction

Twilio (NYSE:TWLO) recently released its Q4 earnings, which were quite disappointing. The management's cautious FY2025 and Q1 guidance reinforce my assertion that the company's organic growth potential peak is far in the rearview window. Inorganic moves to drive business expansion are inherently very risky, which makes TWLO's long-term growth prospects highly uncertain. In addition, the stock is overvalued even after the post-earnings dip, leading to a 'Sell' rating.

Fundamental analysis

Twilio is a technology company which offers a cloud-based platform that helps entities to integrate authentication, messaging, voice, and video functionality directly into their software applications.

Revenue generation primarily relies on a usage-based model, including per-minute rates for voice calls, per-message rates for SMS, and monthly fees for phone numbers. There are also subscription-based services like Email API, Marketing Campaigns, Twilio Flex, and Twilio Segment. In addition, the company generates revenue from premium support services for enterprise clients, partnership and reseller programs, and custom solution development.

The management estimates the current TAM at $86 billion, while Twilio's FY2024 revenue is $4.5 billion. This means that the company's market share is quite modest at 5%. On the other hand, the same presentation from the company suggests that it leads in terms of the market share in communications platform as a service. As shown below, the main competitors are not widely known companies. This means that there are lots of small players in the market, which is risky for TWLO.

The management expects the TAM to deliver an 11% CAGR by 2028. With that said, industry trends are positive for TWLO. On the other hand, a low double-digit CAGR likely indicates that the market is quite mature, and the industry's growth rate might decelerate to single digits after 2028. With the market maturing, and intense competition, it is not surprising that Twilio's revenue growth has plummeted in the last few years.

Furthermore, Wall Street analysts expect next decade's revenue growth to be in single digits. This is notably lower than the projected 11% industry CAGR by the management, meaning that competitive pressures are likely to weigh on TWLO's revenue growth potential.

The latest earnings release does not add optimism at all. The company missed consensus EPS estimates despite delivering a positive revenue surprise. The Q4 revenue growth accelerated to 11% on a year-over-year basis, while full-year growth was at 9%. The adjusted EPS expended from $0.86 to $1.00.

The EPS improvement does not add a lot of optimism because the gross margin has demonstrated sequential decline again despite revenue consistently demonstrating quarter-over-quarter growth. Moreover, the company's first-ever quarter with positive operating income was achieved due to the decreased R&D to revenue ratio, which looks like a questionable decision to me.

The management's Q1 and full-year FY2025 guidance reinforce my cautious long-term outlook. Full-year revenue growth is expected to be between 7% and 8%. For the first quarter, the management forecasts an 8-9% revenue growth. Moreover, the non-GAAP EPS is expected to decline notably on a quarter-over-quarter basis.

As we see from the management's conservative guidance, the company's organic growth potential peak is highly likely in the rearview window. The R&D spending curve also reinforces this assertion. The company's R&D budget has stagnated since late 2022, which might mean that the management does not see much incremental effect on revenue growth potential from additional investments in innovation.

Therefore, the only option to boost revenue growth is an inorganic approach. This is inherently complex and risky because there are several apparent risks. The technological landscape is rapidly evolving, meaning that an acquisition that seems promising and synergetic today may become less relevant within just a few years. There are also risks like high upfront costs, overpaying for an acquisition, and execution risks.

Valuation analysis

Twilio's forward P/E ratio forecast might look bullish at first glance. Due to the projected double-digit EPS growth over the next four years, the FY2028 forward P/E is expected to be as low as 14.8. But there is a big 'but'. The largest portion of the EPS growth is expected in FY2028, which means an extremely high level of uncertainty.

Now, the DCF it is. My CAPM calculations suggest that Twilio's cost of equity is 10.66%. I am using this metric to discount future cash flow instead of WACC because of the company's quite low reliance on debt.

Revenue assumptions for FY2026-2027 are based on consensus, which is an average opinion of at least 13 Wall Street analysts. For FY2028-2030, a steady 25-basis point per year revenue growth decline is forecasted. The TTM levered FCF margin is 16.84%. To balance out my bearish stance and the projected aggressive EPS expansion, I expect the FCF margin to improve by 150-basis points yearly. However, due to all the fundamental risks and uncertainties, the constant growth rate to calculate terminal value ('TV') is 3%. Seeking Alpha suggests there are 153.38 million TWLO shares outstanding.

The fair share price is $116, around 7% lower than the last close. Therefore, TWLO appears to be overvalued even after the post-earnings dip.

Mitigating factors

Wall Street analysts have more optimistic target price estimates, as the average target is $141. Moreover, the stock boasts a 'Strong buy' 4.8 overall rating from SA Quant. Therefore, I might be underestimating TWLO's long-term growth potential. On the other hand, TWLO's consensus target price might not be a very reliable source of information as the stock had a $500 target price in 2021, which TWLO never achieved.

As I mentioned in my fundamental analysis, TWLO has quite modest organic growth potential. Driving inorganic growth is much riskier, as the success of any M&A deal is quite uncertain. There are apparent risks of failing to achieve synergy expectations and massive integration risks. However, Twilio's strong balance sheet with a $1 billion+ net cash position gives the company financial flexibility which might be exercised to finance multiple inorganic growth opportunities and one of them might become a game-changer.

The technical setup looks favorable as well. The RSI indicator sits at around 42, not very far from the 'Oversold' zone. Should the RSI move to the south, this might be a buy signal for bulls and drive the share price up. On the other hand, the chart below suggests that TWLO's RSI indicator can go much deeper before a rebound occurs.

Conclusion

I do not recommend buying the post-earnings dip because TWLO remains overvalued. The company's long-term growth prospects look cloudy, which is reinforced by the recent disappointing earnings release and weak guidance.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Baidu Slips As CEO Missing From Xi Meeting, Q4 Beats
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Earnings Snapshot: Baidu (BIDU) Q4 beats top-line and bottom-line estimates. (00:26) OpenAI said to mull special voting rights to block hostile bids. (01:34) Southwest expands cost-cutting by announcing 15% reduction in corporate overhead workforce. (02:17)

This is an abridged transcript.

Baidu reported earnings today ahead of market open beating top-line and bottom-line estimates for Q4.

Baidu Non-GAAP EPADS of $2.63 beats by $0.78, revenue of $4.67B beats by $70M.

Ahead of the reporting, shares of Baidu in Hong Kong fell about 7% on Monday after CEO Robin Li was not seen at a meeting between China's President Xi Jinping and top corporate executives, Reuters reported.

Shares fell as much as 8.8% before closing down -6.94% to HK$89.850 in Hong Kong. Baidu lost about $2.4B in market value on Monday, according to the report. Shares closed 0.4% higher on Tuesday.

Xi held a meeting in Beijing with the country's top industry tycoons, including Alibaba's (BABA) founder Jack Ma and Huawei's CEO Ren Zhengfei, however, there was no sign of Li attending the meeting, the report added, citing two sources.

Baidu did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

The meeting with Xi indicated the government's support for China's technology sector with an aim to bolster economic growth amid an intensifying China-U.S. technology tussle.

OpenAI is weighing special voting rights for its non-profit board to preserve its directors' decision-making power.

If granted, the special voting rights would allow the non-profit board to overrule major OpenAI investors like Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) and SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY).

Sources told the Financial TimesThe special voting rights are among new governance measures being evaluated by OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and board members as the company transitions to a for-profit structure.

OpenAI last week turned down a $97.4B takeover offer by a consortium led by Elon Musk, who co-founded the company in 2015, but chose to leave in 2018.

After announcing a couple of decisions in January, including reducing pilot head count at a couple of bases and a pause on hiring, Southwest Airlines (NYSE:LUV) on Monday announced slashing about 15% of its corporate jobs or 1,750 people in a cost saving measure.

The workforce reduction of approximately 1,750 roles is focused almost entirely on corporate overhead and leadership positions and represents approximately 15% of corporate positions, including senior leadership and directors. Eleven senior leadership positions (Vice President and above), also representing 15% of the Company's senior management committee, will be eliminated as part of this restructuring. Separations will be substantially complete by the end of second quarter 2025.

The Company estimates partial year 2025 savings to be approximately $210 million and full-year 2026 savings to be approximately $300 million.

The company paused corporate hiring and suspended most of its summer internships in January.

The carrier also reduced pilot positions by 270 at its Denver and Atlanta hubs as part of the carrier’s cost-cutting and aircraft efficiency efforts.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Short sellers reduce bets against information technology stocks in January

Tesla's FSD approval in China could be used for bargaining amid trade tensions - report

South Korea plans to secure 10,000 GPUs for AI computing center - report

Catalyst watch:

  • Notable investor events include the Jacobs Solutions' (J) Investor Day event.

  • The CAGNY Conference will include presentations from Kraft Heinz (KHC), Hershey (HSY), General Mills (GIS), and Coca-Cola (KO).

  • Shareholders with Discover Financial (DFS) and Capital One (COF) will vote on the pending mega-merger between the banking giants.

  • The three-day CoinDesk Consensus Hong Kong event will begin. The crypto conference marks the first expansion of the Consensus event series beyond North America.

  • The quiet period ends on Venture Global (VG) to free up analysts to issue ratings.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 0.8% at nearly $72/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.4% at $95,000.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is up 0.1% and the DAX is down 0.2%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Delta Air Lines (NYSE:DAL) shares slipped over 3% after one of its regional jets lost control upon landing in icy, windy conditions at Toronto Pearson International Airport.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 10:00 am Housing Market Index

  • 10:20 am San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly speaks and participates in a moderated discussion before the 2025 Conference for Community Bankers hosted by the American Bankers Association.

  • 1:00 pm Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr speaks on "Artificial Intelligence in the Economy and Financial Stability" before the Council on Foreign Relations.
