data.title: Amazon: First Peak, Then Pullback
data.description:
While I do not cover all the Mag7 companies [I never really wrote about Tesla (TSLA)], I cover most of them on a rather frequent basis. Right now, I only own Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), which was really cheap about two years ago when I bought it. For most of the Mag7 companies, I certainly acknowledge they are great businesses and most of them have a wide economic moat around them, but I did not invest in them in the last few years.

In retrospect, I missed a few investing opportunities as I clearly did not see what impact ChatGPT would have on NVIDIA’s (NVDA) business in late 2022 and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) as well as Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) were also buying opportunities in late 2022, which I missed. I called Amazon a “Buying Opportunity in the making” and rated Alphabet as a “Buy” and called the stock undervalued.

In my last article about Amazon, I rated the stock as a “Hold” again and saw it still fighting with its resistance level. Shortly after my article was published, Amazon broke above that resistance level and is still on its way towards a higher price. Therefore, let’s take another look at Amazon to answer the question if I am frequently a little too bearish about Amazon and therefore miss out on a great long-term investment.

Technical Picture

Let’s start by looking at the chart. As I have already mentioned above, Amazon broke above the price level I still saw as a resistance level last time. And hand in hand with the overall market, which also performed quite well in the last few months, Amazon was moving higher and gained about 15% since my last article was published.

When looking at the Fibonacci retracement levels we can expect Amazon to increase further and the stock will climb at least to the 1.61 Fibonacci extension, which would push the stock price as high as $255, and it seems not unreasonable at this point for Amazon to climb about 10% higher before a bigger correction is beginning.

Another pattern we see quite often is the pullback to the breakout level, which might lead Amazon’s stock back down to a range of $175 to $190. This might result in a 30% decline from the hypothetical top around $255 and such a decline certainly does not happen every few quarters, but it is also not unreasonable or unlikely.

Quarterly Results

While I pay attention to the chart and certainly believe that the chart is providing us a lot of information about the stock, we should especially look at the fundamental business as it is the most important aspect to determine if a company is a great long-term investment or not.

On February 6, 2025, the company reported fourth-quarter and full-year results for fiscal 2024. And similar to the last few quarters, Amazon did beat estimates for revenue as well as earnings per share again. Aside from beating estimates, Amazon also reported year-over-year growth rates for almost all metrics on the income statement. When looking at the full-year results, total net sales increased from $574,785 million in fiscal 2023 to $637,959 million in fiscal 2024 – resulting in 11.0% year-over-year growth. And while revenue already increased in the double-digits, operating income came close to doubling and grew 86.1% YoY from $36,852 million in fiscal 2023 to $68,593 million in fiscal 2024. And diluted earnings per share even increased 90.7% YoY from $2.90 in fiscal 2023 to $5.53 in fiscal 2024.

These numbers are extremely impressive so far. However, one important number increased with a much lower pace – free cash flow. In fiscal 2023, Amazon generated $36,813 million in FCF and year-over-year it increased only 3.8% to $38,219 million in fiscal 2024.

We can also look at fourth quarter results. Amazon is still reporting great results and growth rates were slightly lower. Total net sales increased from $169,961 million in Q4/23 to $187,792 million in Q4/24 – resulting in 10.5% year-over-year growth. Operating income increased from $13,209 million in Q4/23 to $21,203 million in Q4/24 – resulting in 60.5% year-over-year growth. Diluted earnings per share increased 86.0% YoY from $1.00 in Q4/23 to $1.86 in Q4/24.

Growth

For the next quarter, Amazon expects sales to be between $151 billion and $155.5 billion. Compared to $143.3 billion in the same quarter last year, top line growth will only be between 5% and 8.5%. However, foreign exchange rates might have a negative impact and 2024 being a leap year also had a positive impact on the first quarter.

However, we are not looking at just one quarter, as the information from a single quarter is rather limited when thinking about investing long-term. The answer to the question how Amazon can grow over the long run has two parts. Maybe at some point, Amazon will also start buying back shares, which will also contribute to the bottom-line growth, but for now, the number of outstanding shares is rather increasing and therefore has a slightly negative impact on the bottom line. Therefore, top line growth and improving margins will be the two major contributors to bottom line growth.

On the one hand, Amazon will continue to grow its top line and one of the drivers for growth will be advertising. In the fourth quarter, Amazon generated $17,288 million in advertising revenue, resulting in 18.0% year-over-year growth. The annual run rate is now $69 billion – more than double the run rate of $29 billion four years ago. And while Amazon is generating the biggest part of ad revenue from sponsored products, management is also pleased with the first year of advertising on Prime Video:

On the streaming video side, we wrapped up our first year of prime video ads and we're quite pleased with the early progress and head into this year with momentum.

A second major driver of top line growth is Amazon’s cloud business – the AWS business segment. In Q4/24, revenue increased 18.9% year-over-year to $28,786 million and especially operating income improved at a high pace and increased 48.3% year-over-year to $10,632 million.

But management is expecting growth to be “lumpy” over the next few years – whatever that means:

AWS is a reasonably large business by most folks' standards. And though we expect growth will be lumpy over the next few years as enterprise adoption cycles, capacity considerations and technology advancements impact timing, it's hard to overstate how optimistic we are about what lies ahead for AWS's customers and business.

And while growth might be rather low in the next few years or growth rates might fluctuate more than usual, I think Jassy is right over the long run and generative AI will be found in almost every app and combined with the need for databases or data storage this will drive the demand for cloud solutions. This also goes hand-in-hand with most studies expecting growth in the teens for the global cloud market. Grand View Research, for example, is expecting the U.S. Cloud Computing Market to grow with a CAGR of 20.3% until fiscal 2030. Fortune Business Insights is expecting the Cloud Computing Market to grow with a CAGR of16.5% until fiscal 2032. And Precedence Research is expecting even a CAGR of 21.2% until fiscal 2034. And finally, IDC is expecting a CAGR of 19.4% for the next four years until fiscal 2028.

Aside from top line growth, Amazon is also quite optimistic that improved efficiency will also contribute to bottom line growth and Amazon will probably continue to improve its margins.

During the earnings call, CEO Andy Jassy commented for example on the improved US network, which is not only improving speed of delivery (the metro areas with same-day delivery increased by 60%), but also increasing efficiency:

We've talked about the regionalization of our US network. We've also recently rolled out our redesigned US inbound network. While still in its early stages, our inbound efforts have improved our placement of inventory so that even more items are closer to end customers. Ahead of Black Friday in November, we'd improved the percentage of ordered units available in the ideal building by over 40% year-over-year.

And he continued with further potential to reduce costs in 2025:

As we look to 2025 and beyond, we see opportunity to reduce costs again as we further refine inventory placement, grow our same-day delivery network and accelerate robotics and automation throughout the network.

Although this is only one example, I assume that Amazon has the potential to increase efficiency in the years to come, and this will certainly play a role in bottom line growth. Of course, at some point the contribution of lowered costs will not have such a huge impact on bottom line growth anymore and long-term growth (over decades) has to stem from revenue growth.

But right now, we also see capital expenditures being extremely high and during the last earnings call, CFO Brian Olsavsky commented on the expectations for fiscal 2025:

Now turning to our capital investments. As a reminder, we define these as a combination of cash CapEx plus equipment finance leases. Capital investments were $26.3 billion in the fourth quarter, and we think that run rate will be reasonably representative of our 2025 capital investment rate. Similar to 2024, the majority of the spend will be to support the growing need for technology infrastructure. This primarily relates to AWS, including to support demand for our AI services as well as tech infrastructure to support our North America and International segments.

When looking at the trailing twelve months numbers, Amazon spent $83 billion on capital expenditures, which is resulting in 13.0% of revenue. And while CapEx (as percentage of revenue) was higher for Amazon in 2000 and other big tech companies have a higher spendings ratio right now – like Meta Platforms and Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) – we see capital expenditures constantly increasing in the last two decades for Amazon.

We can speculate that capital expenditures might be lower again at some point. But at least for the next few quarters, spendings will be extremely high due to investments in AI infrastructure (mostly for AWS). When asked about capital expenditures during the earnings call, CEO Andy Jassy commented:

The vast majority of that CapEx spend is on AI for AWS. It's -- the way the AWS business works and the way the cash cycle works is that the faster we grow, the more CapEx we end up spending because we have to procure data center and hardware and chips and networking gear ahead of when we're able to monetize it.

Overall, analysts are rather optimistic for the years to come and expect earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 11.4%. Aside from expecting double-digit growth, we also see – by looking at the earnings revision data – that analysts were getting much more optimistic and constantly increased targets for the next few years. This is also a good sign for the business improving.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

We can start by looking at the simple valuation metrics – especially the price-earnings ratio and the price-free-cash-flow ratio. At the time of writing, the price-earnings ratio was 42 and therefore clearly below the 5-year average of 85.08. Additionally, Amazon is trading at a price-free-cash-flow ratio of 76 right now. We already mentioned above that capital expenditures are rather high for Amazon. And as long as we assume that capital expenditures (especially as percentage of revenue) will be lower again at some point, we should question if the P/FCF ratio is a good metric right now. But leaving these points aside, the valuation multiple is extremely high and rather hinting towards Amazon being expensive.

I have also mentioned several times in the past that a discount cash flow calculation is the much better way to determine an intrinsic value for a stock and answer the question if a stock might be over- or undervalued. When calculating an intrinsic value by using a discount cash flow calculation, we calculate with 10,771 million shares outstanding and a 10% discount rate (as this is the annual return we like to achieve).

While these assumptions are rather obvious, it now gets much more difficult. I usually take the free cash flow of the last four quarters – in case of Amazon $32,880 million. Additionally, we are calculating with a 4% growth rate till perpetuity – as we should according to CFI Institute. In this scenario, Amazon has to grow 27% annually for the next ten years, which does not seem reasonable and achievable for Amazon.

But we can be a little more optimistic. First, we can discuss the 4% growth till perpetuity assumption and Amazon is most likely able to grow with a much higher rate than 4% in 10, 20 or 30 years from now. In this case, Amazon would only have to grow 22% for the next 10 years. In my opinion, still a rather high growth rates for Amazon and clearly above analysts’ assumptions.

Additionally, we can argue how realistic the trailing twelve months free cash flow is as basis at this point. Despite high expectations for capital expenditures, which will make it difficult to generate a huge amount of free cash flow in fiscal 2025, we must be optimistic for free cash flow over the long run and the current free cash flow to revenue ratio is rather low.

When assuming $50 billion in free cash flow as basis for our calculation (which seems in reach for Amazon when CapEx is lower), Amazon must grow 16% for the next ten years to be fairly valued. And while 16% growth seems achievable, I would definitely not argue that Amazon is undervalued here, and I would also question any statement about Amazon being fairly valued right now.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Amazon breaking out above its previous resistance level can be seen as bullish and the stock might increase a bit higher from higher. But from a fundamental perspective, Amazon’s valuation is very stretched, and a correction seems likely. Although I have been wrong in the past and Amazon would have been a solid investment since my last article, I will stay to the sidelines at this point.

data.title: LGLV: A Tactical Play For Low-Volatility Exposure With Growth Potential
data.description:
ETF Overview

SPDR SSGA US Large Cap Low Volatility Index ETF (NYSEARCA:LGLV) invests in a portfolio of about 162 large-cap low volatility stocks. The fund has a low expense ratio of 0.12%. Other funds such as iShares MSCI USA Min Vol Factor ETF (USMV) has a slightly higher expense ratio of 0.15%. LGLV follows a unique weighting methodology that assigns higher weights to stocks with lower volatility, rather than using a traditional market-cap weighting approach. This results in a portfolio tilted towards mid-cap and large-cap value stocks. LGLV has a better growth profile than other low volatility funds such as Invesco S&P 500 Low Volatility ETF (SPLV) thanks to its higher exposure to technology and lower exposure to defensive sectors. While LGLV has selected stocks with lower volatility, it can still occasionally lead to higher downside risk due to sector-specific risk. Given these characteristics, LGLV is best suited as a tactical holding rather than a core holding for investors seeking exposure to lower volatility stocks.

Fund Analysis

LGLV’s portfolio construction methodology

Let us begin our analysis by reviewing how LGLV constructs its portfolio. The fund seeks to track the SSGA US Large Cap Low Volatility Index. This index selects the least volatile 30% within each sector of the 1,000 largest U.S. stocks by market capitalization. Weightings are not market-cap weighted. Instead, stocks with lower volatility receives higher weighting. LGLV rebalances its portfolio annually in March every year.

The result is a portfolio that is tilted towards value. As can be seen from the chart below, growth stocks only represent about 8% of LGLV’s portfolio. In contrast, 31% of the portfolio belong to value category. LGLV’s lower exposure to growth is understandable as growth stocks generally have higher volatilities, especially due to their elevated valuations. Because LGLV is not weighted by market-cap, the fund is not overwhelmingly large-cap. Instead, large-cap and mid-cap stocks represent about 45% and 47% of LGLV’s portfolio, respectively. On the other hand, small-cap stocks make up only 8% of the portfolio.

LGLV has outperformed SPLV

Let us now compare LGLV’s performance with SPLV, a fund that selects low volatility stocks from the S&P 500 index. As can be seen from the chart below, LGLV delivered a total return of 190.5% in the past 10 years, outperforming SPLV’s total return of 134.3%.

The difference is due to their sector compositions

Why is there such a big difference between LGLV and SPLV’s performance? Well, it is likely due to their sector composition differences. Below is a table that shows LGLV’s sector allocation. As can be seen from the table, technology sector represents about 12.7% of LGLV’s portfolio. In contrast, technology sector only represents just about 9.0% of SPLV’s portfolio. As we know, technology sector has been one of the fastest growing sectors among all other sectors in the past. This sector is poised to deliver strong growth in the upcoming decade thanks to many technological megatrends such as AI, cloud computing, robotics, etc. From this perspective, LGLV appears to have a better growth profile than SPLV.

In addition to higher exposure to technology, LGLV’s exposure to defensive sectors is not as high as SPLV’s exposure. As the table above shows, defensive sectors such as consumer defensive, healthcare, and utilities represent about 24.6% of LGLV’s portfolio. In contrast, these defensive sectors account for a significantly larger share of SPLV’s portfolio at 39.2%. Defensive sectors such as consumer defensive and utilities sectors are not fast-growing sectors. Stocks from these sectors usually have slower growth rates than large-cap stocks. Therefore, SPLV’s higher exposure weakens its performance outlook.

Generally lower downside risk, but not in 2020

LGLV has an average 5-year monthly beta ratio of 0.76 relative to the S&P 500 index. For reader’s information, the beta ratio measures the fund’s price volatility. While LGLV has lower volatility than the broader market, it does not mean it has lower downside risk in every market turmoil. In the past 10 years, there were four major market corrections: the correction in 2016, 2019, 2020, and 2022. LGLV has proven to deliver lower downside risk than the S&P 500 index in 2016, 2019, and 2022. However, during the bear market triggered by the pandemic in 2020, LGLV has fallen harder than the S&P 500 index. Why would this happen? Well, in the 2020s pandemic-driven bear market, sectors such as industrials and financial services were hit hard. LGLV happened to have higher exposure to these two sectors than the S&P 500 index. For reader’s information, LGLV’s exposure to these two sectors is currently around 33.1% whereas the S&P 500 index’s exposure is only 20.8%. Investors should keep in mind that LGLV does not always provide better downside protection than the broader market.

Investor Takeaway

LGLV’s higher exposure to technology and lower allocation to defensive sectors has contributed to its outperformance relative to SPLV. However, investors should be aware that LGLV is not immune to market downturns and can still expose itself to sector-specific risk. Therefore, this fund is better suited as a tactical play rather than a long-term core holding for investors seeking exposure to low-volatility large-cap and mid-cap stocks.

Additional Disclosure: This is not financial advice and that all financial investments carry risks. Investors are expected to seek financial advice from professionals before making any investment.
